¶

Flow-Based Network Intrusion Detection (Multiclass Classification) — Machine Learning Study¶

Author: Luis Rito Filipe¶

July, 2023¶


Summary¶

This study evaluates multiple machine learning models for network intrusion detection, formulated as a multiclass classification problem, with the objective of maximizing malicious traffic detection while minimizing false positives.

Among the evaluated models, LightGBM was selected as the final model, as it consistently achieved high performance across all evaluation metrics and satisfied the defined constraint (Recall ≥ 0.99). While differences between the top-performing models are minimal, LightGBM provides a favorable balance between recall and precision under threshold tuning, aligning with the objective of maximizing malicious traffic detection while maintaining acceptable false positive rates.

The results further indicate that malicious traffic detection is largely driven by a limited subset of flow-based features. Despite variations in feature importance across models, all approaches achieve near-perfect classification performance, suggesting that the dataset exhibits strong intrinsic separability in the selected feature space.

About The Study¶

The objective of this study is to compare machine learning algorithms when applied to data analysis to detect malicious traffic. To achieve this, I will leverage widely used Python libraries specialized in data analysis and machine learning, including tools for data manipulation, visualization, and predictive modeling. The study will progress from exploratory data analysis to more advanced techniques, such as feature engineering and fraud detection modeling, to provide meaningful insights into the characteristics of malicious traffic detection. This study follows the binary classification study applied to the same dataset (Flow-based network intrusion (binary classification)).

About The Dataset¶

The following study uses a dataset of network intrusion publicly available from Kaggle (see link below). The dataset was produced by Canadian Institute for Cybersecurity.

Dataset link: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/chethuhn/network-intrusion-dataset

Data Wrangling¶

Two datasets with exact the same 79 features, one with DDoS attacks and other with PortScan-related traffic, were used. After loading and merging the datasets, data was prepared.

In this study only a subset from the 79 initial features was used (Flow Bytes/s, Flow Packets/s, Fwd Packets/s, Bwd Packets/s, Subflow Fwd Packets, Subflow Fwd Bytes, Packet Length Std, Packet Length Variance, Flow Duration, Init_Win_bytes_forward, Init_Win_bytes_backward, Down/Up Ratio, Label).

There are 10 features with only one unique value, which imply they have zero variance and cannot help distinguishing Label 0 vs Label 1. Mathematically, for models like logistic regression, the coefficient becomes meaningless and it may even cause numerical instability in some cases. If any of these were included in the subset used, it would also be removed.

Label feature contains three unique values, namely "BENIGN", "DDoS" and "PortScan", which will be encoded to integers. Label feature was renamed to Class. A space before or after each feature name was removed. A few rows with infinite values were removed. Rows with values of -1 in features "Init_Win_bytes_forward" and "Init_Win_bytes_backward" were removed as this is an indication that the information was not available or not applicable for a particular flow. 

{'BENIGN': np.int64(0), 'DDoS': np.int64(1), 'PortScan': np.int64(2)}

array([106407,  81497, 158740])

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Feature correlation analysis helped identify strongly correlated feature pairs, allowing for the removal of redundant variables and improving model robustness. Below, the summary statistics of the dataset reduced and prepared for the machine learning algorithms.

The ANOVA F-test in the following chart indicates that Packet Length Std is one of the most discriminative features in the dataset, showing a significantly higher score compared to all other features, suggesting this feature strongly separates normal traffic from DDoS attacks and PortScan-related traffic. This result is supported by the boxplot analysis.

Boxplot analysis highlights strong separation of classes by a small set of features. Class 0 Packet Length Std exhibits a wide spread with numerous outliers, indicating high variability in normal traffic behavior, while class 1 (DDoS) shows a much tighter distribution with minimal overlap with Class 0, suggesting more consistent packet length variation during malicious traffic, and class 2 (PortScan) is heavily concentrated in a narrow range near the lower end of the scale, with a compressed interquartile range and limited spread, indicating a distinctly different traffic pattern compared to the other classes.

Class 0 Init_Win_bytes_forward also exhibits a wide spread with numerous outliers, indicating high variability in normal traffic behavior. Class 1 (DDoS) shows a highly compressed distribution with low outliers, while class 2 (PortScan) exhibits minimal overlap with class 1 and a wider interquartile.


In the next step, the dataset is split into training and testing sets to allow proper training of the classification models and unbiased evaluation of their performance. 
X_train.shape= (232251, 9) Y_train.shape= (232251,)
X_test.shape= (114393, 9) Y_test.shape= (114393,)

Logistic Regression

Logistic Regression requires additional considerations when applied to multiclass problems. While the binary case involves a single decision boundary, the multiclass scenario requires the model to distinguish between multiple classes simultaneously, increasing the complexity of the optimization problem.

In this study, the multiclass formulation was handled using the default multinomial approach with the lbfgs solver. This approach optimizes a single objective function across all classes, rather than training independent one-vs-rest classifiers, and is generally more appropriate when classes are not linearly separable.

Class imbalance was addressed through the use of class weights. However, unlike in the binary case, where adjusting weights directly controls the trade-off between false positives and false negatives, in the multiclass setting the impact of class weighting is distributed across multiple decision boundaries. As a result, tuning class weights becomes less intuitive and may lead to less stable improvements. Despite these adjustments, Logistic Regression underperformed compared to tree-based ensemble methods.

Accuracy PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score F1Weighted MCC TP_0 FP_0 FN_0 TN_0 TP_1 FP_1 FN_1 TN_1 TP_2 FP_2 FN_2 TN_2
0 0.459845 0.518012 0.508632 0.452846 0.455136 0.245986 5743 32291 29372 46987 25855 27673 1039 59826 21005 1826 31379 60183

The following table shows the computed feature coefficients ordered from highest to lowest.
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Subflow Fwd Packets Subflow Fwd Bytes Down/Up Ratio Init_Win_bytes_forward Init_Win_bytes_backward Flow Bytes/s Flow Packets/s Flow Duration Packet Length Std
Coefficient 33.57 26.346 2.822 2.363 1.009 0.095 -0.028 -0.757 -7.666

A confusion matrix is shown below for the model predictions obtained using the standard multiclass decision rule, where each observation is assigned to the class with the highest predicted probability.

Random Forest

Random Forest requires fewer structural changes when moving from binary to multiclass classification, as it natively supports multiple classes without requiring a reformulation of the learning objective. The same impurity criteria (e.g., Gini or entropy) are used to evaluate splits across all classes, and each tree learns to separate the full label space rather than a binary decision boundary.

In this study, the main adjustment concerned the handling of class imbalance through the class_weight parameter, which in the multiclass setting distributes weighting across several positive classes rather than a single positive class.

Additionally, prediction in the multiclass case relies on aggregating class probabilities across all trees and selecting the class with the highest averaged probability, which can amplify the impact of poorly represented classes if not properly balanced. Apart from these considerations, the model configuration remained largely unchanged, reinforcing Random Forest’s suitability as a robust baseline for multiclass intrusion detection tasks.

Accuracy PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score F1Weighted MCC TP_0 FP_0 FN_0 TN_0 TP_1 FP_1 FN_1 TN_1 TP_2 FP_2 FN_2 TN_2
0 0.999878 0.999868 0.999868 0.999868 0.999878 0.999809 35111 10 4 79268 26889 2 5 87497 52379 2 5 62007

The following table shows the computed feature importance ordered from highest to lowest.
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Subflow Fwd Bytes Packet Length Std Flow Packets/s Flow Duration Init_Win_bytes_backward Subflow Fwd Packets Init_Win_bytes_forward Flow Bytes/s Down/Up Ratio
Importance 0.318 0.229 0.159 0.108 0.092 0.058 0.02 0.016 0.002

There is a noticeable difference in the importance attributed to features "Subflow Fwd Bytes" by each algorithm. "Subflow Fwd Bytes" measures how much forward data is transmitted within segments of a flow, providing insight into how traffic volume is distributed rather than just the total amount. A subflow is a segment of a larger flow. Malicious traffic flows (Class 1) tend to send less data forward per segment. This feature may reveal important patterns that can be captured by tree models such as Random Forest.

A confusion matrix is shown below for the model predictions obtained using the standard multiclass decision rule, where each observation is assigned to the class with the highest predicted probability.

XGBoost

XGBoost requires more explicit adaptation when transitioning from binary to multiclass classification, as the objective function must be redefined to handle multiple output classes simultaneously.

The XGBoost classifier was configured for multiclass learning using the multi:softprob objective and optimized via log-loss. Binary-specific balancing parameters such as scale_pos_weight were not used, as they are not applicable in a multiclass setting. Instead, model performance was controlled through hyperparameter tuning of tree complexity, learning rate, and subsampling ratios to mitigate overfitting and improve generalization across heterogeneous intrusion classes.

Accuracy PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score F1Weighted MCC TP_0 FP_0 FN_0 TN_0 TP_1 FP_1 FN_1 TN_1 TP_2 FP_2 FN_2 TN_2
0 0.999895 0.999887 0.999905 0.999896 0.999895 0.999836 35109 6 6 79272 26894 3 0 87496 52378 3 6 62006

The following table shows the computed feature importance from highest to lowest.
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Subflow Fwd Bytes Packet Length Std Init_Win_bytes_backward Flow Duration Flow Bytes/s Init_Win_bytes_forward Flow Packets/s Subflow Fwd Packets Down/Up Ratio
Importance 1189.0 685.0 414.0 216.0 184.0 95.0 66.0 60.0 37.0

A confusion matrix is shown below for the model predictions obtained using the standard multiclass decision rule, where each observation is assigned to the class with the highest predicted probability.

LightGBM

Unlike binary classification, no direct equivalent of scale_pos_weight is used; instead, class imbalance was handled through class or sample weighting to ensure fair representation of minority intrusion types during split optimization. Given LightGBM’s histogram-based learning strategy, such weighting is particularly important to prevent majority classes from dominating tree structure formation.

Accuracy PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score F1Weighted MCC TP_0 FP_0 FN_0 TN_0 TP_1 FP_1 FN_1 TN_1 TP_2 FP_2 FN_2 TN_2
0 0.999834 0.999823 0.999821 0.999822 0.999834 0.999741 35108 12 7 79266 26888 3 6 87496 52378 4 6 62005

The following table shows the computed feature importance from highest to lowest.
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Subflow Fwd Bytes Packet Length Std Flow Bytes/s Init_Win_bytes_forward Init_Win_bytes_backward Flow Packets/s Down/Up Ratio Subflow Fwd Packets Flow Duration
Importance 6427796.0 4890752.0 748749.0 157324.0 120509.0 41977.0 16389.0 6769.0 3072.0

A confusion matrix is shown below for the model predictions obtained using the standard multiclass decision rule, where each observation is assigned to the class with the highest predicted probability.

CatBoost

CatBoost was applied using its native multiclass learning capability. Unlike XGBoost and LightGBM, CatBoost does not rely on binary-oriented weighting parameters such as scale_pos_weight; instead, class imbalance can be optionally addressed through auto_class_weights="Balanced". However, due to CatBoost’s ordered boosting mechanism, the model is generally more robust to class imbalance and reduces the need for extensive weight adjustments. Performance was evaluated using macro-averaged metrics and MCC to ensure fair assessment across all classes.

Accuracy PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score F1Weighted MCC TP_0 FP_0 FN_0 TN_0 TP_1 FP_1 FN_1 TN_1 TP_2 FP_2 FN_2 TN_2
0 0.998663 0.998589 0.998769 0.998679 0.998663 0.997913 35079 92 36 79186 26870 25 24 87474 52291 36 93 61973

The following table shows the computed feature importance from highest to lowest.
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
Feature Subflow Fwd Bytes Packet Length Std Init_Win_bytes_backward Init_Win_bytes_forward Flow Duration Flow Packets/s Flow Bytes/s Subflow Fwd Packets Down/Up Ratio
Importance 29.0 29.0 18.0 6.0 5.0 4.0 4.0 4.0 2.0

A confusion matrix is shown below for the model predictions obtained using the standard multiclass decision rule, where each observation is assigned to the class with the highest predicted probability.

Model Comparison and Selection

The next step is to define the desired model outcome for the multiclass intrusion detection task. Unlike binary classification, where the focus is on distinguishing between malicious traffic and normal traffic, the multiclass setting requires the model to correctly differentiate between normal traffic (BENIGN) and multiple malicious traffics (DDoS and PortScan). As a result, overall accuracy alone is not sufficient to evaluate performance, since it may hide poor performance on individual classes.

In this context, model selection is performed by first enforcing a class-specific recall, particularly for the malicious traffic categories (DDoS and PortScan), to ensure that malicious traffic is not missed. Among the models that satisfy these minimum recall requirements, the final selection is based on the best overall balance of F1Score and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), as these metrics provide a robust assessment of both class-wise performance and global predictive quality. This approach ensures that the selected model achieves strong detection performance across all malicious traffic types while maintaining a low rate of misclassification.

This selection strategy reflects a practical decision framework for multiclass security systems, where reliable detection of all malicious traffic categories is prioritized over raw accuracy, and performance is optimized under constraints that reflect operational security requirements.

Model Accuracy PrecisionScore RecallScore F1Score F1Weighted MCC TP_BENIGN FP_BENIGN FN_BENIGN TN_BENIGN TP_DDoS FP_DDoS FN_DDoS TN_DDoS TP_PortScan FP_PortScan FN_PortScan TN_PortScan Recall_DDoS Recall_PortScan LogLoss BrierScoreLoss
0 XGBoost 0.999904 0.999893 0.999914 0.999904 0.999904 0.999850 35110 6 5 79272 26894 3 0 87496 52378 2 6 62007 1.000000 0.999885 0.000484 0.000168
1 Random Forest 0.999878 0.999868 0.999868 0.999868 0.999878 0.999809 35111 10 4 79268 26889 2 5 87497 52379 2 5 62007 0.999814 0.999905 0.001286 0.000267
2 LightGBM 0.999834 0.999823 0.999821 0.999822 0.999834 0.999741 35108 12 7 79266 26888 3 6 87496 52378 4 6 62005 0.999777 0.999885 0.004272 0.000234
3 CatBoost 0.998663 0.998589 0.998769 0.998679 0.998663 0.997913 35079 92 36 79186 26870 25 24 87474 52291 36 93 61973 0.999108 0.998225 0.010123 0.002508

The performance comparison of the evaluated models shows consistently high results across tree-based ensemble methods, with XGBoost, Random Forest, LightGBM, and CatBoost all achieving near-perfect classification performance on the multiclass intrusion detection task. In contrast, Logistic Regression performs significantly worse across all aggregate metrics, confirming that linear decision boundaries are insufficient to capture the complexity of network traffic patterns in this dataset.

Among all models, XGBoost achieves the best overall performance, obtaining the highest values for F1Score, Recall, and MCC, while also maintaining the lowest LogLoss and Brier Score Loss. This indicates not only strong classification accuracy but also well-calibrated probability estimates and robust separation between classes.

From a security perspective, all tree-based models demonstrate extremely high recall for the attack classes (DDoS and PortScan), which is a critical requirement in intrusion detection systems. XGBoost and LightGBM both achieve perfect or near-perfect recall for DDoS traffic, while Random Forest and CatBoost show slightly lower but still highly competitive performance.

Although the performance differences between the top models are marginal, XGBoost is selected as the final model due to its superior balance across all evaluation criteria, particularly its combination of highest F1Score, highest MCC, and lowest probabilistic error (LogLoss and Brier Score). This indicates that it not only performs well in classification accuracy but also provides the most reliable probability estimates among the evaluated models.

Overall, the results confirm that ensemble tree-based methods are significantly more suitable for multiclass network intrusion detection than linear models, with XGBoost providing the most stable and well-rounded performance under the defined evaluation framework.


The confusion matrix shows that the model achieves near-perfect classification across all three classes.

BENIGN traffic is correctly classified in 99.99% of cases, with only a negligible number of misclassifications into malicious traffic classes. DDoS attacks are detected with perfect recall (100%), indicating that no DDoS instances are missed. PortScan malicious traffic is also identified with near-perfect accuracy (99.99%), with only a minimal number of instances incorrectly classified as BENIGN.

Importantly, there is no confusion between malicious traffic classes, meaning that DDoS and PortScan are not misclassified as each other. This is particularly relevant in a cybersecurity context, where distinguishing between different malicious traffic types is essential for appropriate response. Overall, the confusion matrix confirms that the model satisfies the primary requirement of maximizing malicious traffic detection while maintaining extremely low false positive and false negative rates.

The calibration curve evaluates how well the predicted probabilities reflect the true likelihood of each class. For DDoS, the curve closely follows the diagonal reference line, indicating that predicted probabilities are well calibrated and align closely with observed frequencies. For PortScan, the curve shows more variability, particularly in intermediate probability ranges. This suggests that the model tends to produce more extreme probability estimates (i.e., predictions are often close to 0 or 1), with limited representation in mid-confidence regions.

Despite this variability, both classes exhibit strong alignment with the ideal calibration line at higher probability values, which is the most relevant region for decision-making in this context. Overall, the model demonstrates good calibration, particularly for high-confidence predictions, indicating that its probability outputs can be considered reliable for practical use.

Feature Importance Analysis

To better understand the behavior of the selected model, a feature importance analysis was conducted. This step aims to identify which variables contribute most to the model’s predictions and to assess whether these contributions are consistent with the patterns observed during the exploratory data analysis.

The analysis reveals that a small subset of features dominates the model’s decision-making process. In particular, Subflow Fwd Bytes and Packet Length Std stand out as the most influential features across the top-performing models, with importance scores significantly higher than all other variables. This dominance indicates that traffic volume and variability in packet size are critical factors in distinguishing between traffic classes.

This finding is consistent with earlier observations from the ANOVA analysis and boxplot exploration, where BENIGN traffic exhibited a wide spread with numerous outliers, DDoS traffic showed a more compact distribution with minimal overlap, and PortScan traffic was highly concentrated in lower value ranges. The clear separation in feature distributions across classes explains why these variables provide such strong discriminative power.

Error Analysis

Although the selected model achieves near-perfect classification performance, a closer inspection of the confusion matrix provides insight into the few remaining misclassifications. This analysis helps identify potential edge cases and assess whether the observed errors pose any practical concern.

The number of misclassifications is extremely low across all classes. A small number of BENIGN samples are incorrectly classified as malicious traffic (both DDoS and PortScan). A few PortScan instances are misclassified as BENIGN. No DDoS samples are misclassified, indicating perfect detection for this class. Importantly, there is no confusion between malicious traffic classes, meaning DDoS and PortScan are never mistaken for one another.

The observed errors follow a consistent pattern: Misclassifications occur primarily between BENIGN and PortScan, suggesting that some PortScan traffic shares characteristics with normal traffic. The absence of confusion between DDoS and PortScan indicates that these malicious traffic types are well separated in the feature space, allowing the model to distinguish them reliably. The perfect classification of DDoS suggests that this class exhibits highly distinctive patterns, making it easier to detect.

Conclusion

This study developed and evaluated a multiclass classification model for flow-based network intrusion detection, with the objective of accurately distinguishing between BENIGN traffic, DDoS attacks and PortScan activity.

The analysis showed that all evaluated models achieved near-perfect performance across standard metrics. As a result, model selection was guided not only by overall performance but also by operational constraints, particularly the requirement to maintain high recall for malicious traffic classes. Under these criteria, XGBoost was selected as the final model, as it provided the best balance between classification performance and robustness.

The evaluation of the selected model confirmed its effectiveness. The confusion matrix demonstrated near-perfect classification with negligible misclassification and no confusion between malicious traffic types. The calibration analysis further showed that predicted probabilities are well aligned with observed outcomes, particularly in high-confidence regions, indicating reliable probabilistic outputs.

Feature importance analysis revealed that a small number of variables, notably Subflow Fwd Bytes and Packet Length Std, play a dominant role in the model’s decisions. These findings are consistent with earlier statistical analysis and reflect meaningful patterns in network traffic behavior.

Finally, error analysis confirmed that the few remaining misclassifications are limited to edge cases, primarily involving borderline distinctions between BENIGN and PortScan traffic, with no impact on the detection of DDoS attacks.

Overall, the selected model demonstrates high predictive performance, strong reliability, and clear interpretability. These characteristics make it a suitable candidate for deployment in real-world intrusion detection systems, where accurate malicious traffic detection and trustworthy probability estimates are essential.
